prompt: Write four absurd but coherent sentences in the voice of a confused medieval chronicler who has been hired to document the Q3 results of a modern SaaS company.
response:
I, a humble chronicler in motley and ink-stained sleeve, was summoned to set down the Q3 reckonings of a curious guild called SaaS—neither merchants nor masons, they vend invisible spells on monthly parchments named "subscriptions," and I fancied "MRR" to be the mournful cry of a mermaid.  
Their ledgers avow that "ARR," the yearly recurring tithe, hath swollen like a beached whale, yet there is also "churn," which I at first mistook for the churning of butter but soon learned means tenants and clients fleeing like rats from a sinking barge.  
There be talk of "conversion rates" and fearsome "funnels" through which many a user falleth, and men called "growth hackers" are summoned—folk who hack at numbers as woodcutters do with oak, performing arcane rites such as A/B tests to coax more souls to the banner.  
The final pronouncement spake of "net retention" and made the accountants clap their hands; I, quill trembling beneath my circlet, could only conclude that if customers be steeds and revenue the joust, then Q3 saw new steeds bred, old nags reclaimed, and some unaccountably spirited away by goblins of competition and that dread beast named Churn.
